coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots obtained by unfolding fullerene
coredata.dc:creator: Kaciulis, S.
Mezzi, A.
Soltani, P.
Pizzoferrato, R.
Ciotta, E.
Prosposito, P.
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have recently been proposed as fluorescent sensors for metal ions dissolved in water. The physical origin of this fluorescence is still not completely clear, therefore further studies on GQDs and their properties are required. Graphene-like quantum dots were prepared by oxidation and unfolding of commercial buckminsterfullerene. This process was carried out by a modified Hummers method. After dialysis, the solution was diluted with deionized water to obtain the reference solution for optical measurements. Part of the solution was dried to obtain the unfolded fullerene powder. The metal ions were allowed to interact with GQDs by adding the metal-salt water solution to the stock solution of unfolded fullerene. The properties of GQDs before and after incorporation of metal ions (Hg, Cd, Pb, Cu, Ni and As) were characterized by using X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS), Auger electron spectroscopy, UV–vis and fluorescent spectroscopies. In XPS, a particular attention was given to the analysis of photoemission C 1 s and Auger C KVV spectra in order to determine the carbon electronic configuration and its changes after incorporation of metal ions into GQDs. Obtained quantum dots were composed of graphene with oxidized borders. Optical measurements showed that the interaction with metal ions was changing the fluorescence intensity and optical absorbance, with different characteristics for each distinct ionic species. This behavior could be used for a selective multiple sensor detecting different heavy metals in water solutions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Quantum dots
Fullerene
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy
Auger electron spectroscopy
Optical spectroscopy
Optical sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of GQDs (reproduced with permission from [13]).
AFM image of GQDs (reproduced with permission from [14]).
C 1 s spectra of GQDs sample (a) and GQDs sample with Cu (b): C1 – CC bonds, C2 – CO bonds, C3 – carboxyls.
Auger C KVV spectra of GQDs sample.
Valence band spectra of GQDs samples: pure and with Cu2+.
O 1 s spectra of GQDs samples: pure and with Cu2+. O1 – hydroxyl groups; O2 – carboxyl groups and adsorbed H2O.
Atomic ratios of oxygen species O2: O1 (a) and of metal with main carbon M: C1 (b) in the samples of GQDs with different metal ions.
Core level spectra of As 3d (a) and Cu 2p (b) bonded with GQDs.

Optical emission (a) and UV–vis absorption (b) spectra of GQDs samples: pure GQDs and with different concentrations of Cu2+. Fig. 7a is reproduced with permission from [13], whereas Fig. 7b is partially reproduced from supplementary information in [13].
Optical emission (a) and UV–vis absorption (b) spectra of GQDs samples: pure GQDs and with different concentrations of Cd2+. These results are reproduced with permission from [14].
Values of D parameter for different samples of GQDs with metal ions and for reference samples [12].

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots obtained by unfolding fullerene

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for the Study of Nanostructured Materials, ISMN-CNR, 00015 Rome, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for the Study of Nanostructured Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Industrial Engineering, University of Rome Tor Vergata, 00133 Rome, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have recently been proposed as fluorescent sensors for metal ions dissolved in water. The physical origin of this fluorescence is still not completely clear, therefore further studies on GQDs and their properties are required. Graphene-like quantum dots were prepared by oxidation and unfolding of commercial buckminsterfullerene. This process was carried out by a modified Hummers method. After dialysis, the solution was diluted with deionized water to obtain the reference solution for optical measurements. Part of the solution was dried to obtain the unfolded fullerene powder. The metal ions were allowed to interact with GQDs by adding the metal-salt water solution to the stock solution of unfolded fullerene. The properties of GQDs before and after incorporation of metal ions (Hg, Cd, Pb, Cu, Ni and As) were characterized by using X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS), Auger electron spectroscopy, UV–vis and fluorescent spectroscopies. In XPS, a particular attention was given to the analysis of photoemission C 1 s and Auger C KVV spectra in order to determine the carbon electronic configuration and its changes after incorporation of metal ions into GQDs. Obtained quantum dots were composed of graphene with oxidized borders. Optical measurements showed that the interaction with metal ions was changing the fluorescence intensity and optical absorbance, with different characteristics for each distinct ionic species. This behavior could be used for a selective multiple sensor detecting different heavy metals in water solutions.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) belong to the growing family of carbon nanomaterials with unique properties due to quantum confinement effect, which is opening the bandgap in semimetal graphene. The most remarkable properties of this zero-dimensional material are biocompatibility, strong photoluminescence, hydrophilicity, also high photo-stability and chemical inertness. Because of these properties, the GQDs are very attractive for different fields of application, such as optical sensing [1,2], catalysis [3,4], photovoltaics [5], medical radiotherapy [6], bioimaging [7] and even the drug therapy for neurological disorders including Parkinson's disease [8].

Among the available methods of GQDs preparation (chemical ablation or mechanical grinding of graphene, chemical synthesis, etc.), the most simple and straightforward one is the unfolding of fullerene by using a mixture of strong acid and chemical oxidants [4,9]. Starting from buckminsterfullerene C60 with a diameter of ≈ 1 nm and using the modified Hummers method for its cage-opening, it is possible to obtain well-defined GQDs of about 2–3 nm. Of course, the generated QDs can be highly oxidized to graphene oxide (GO), but the usage of subsequent reduction treatments can yield almost perfect GQDs with oxidized borders [9]. Therefore, a reliable analytical technique capable not only to determine the size of QDs, but to distinguish between the presence of GO and graphene, is needed for the characterization of prepared nanomaterial. Mostly used technique for identification of graphene is Raman spectroscopy, but in the case of QDs the 2D band in Raman spectra is almost absent due to the disruption of 2D structure by the presence of oxidized borders [2]. Another non-destructive technique suitable for identification of graphene is Auger electron spectroscopy (AES), especially when it is combined with X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS). The signal of C 1 s component corresponding to carbon bonded to oxygen is high for GO and becomes low in the samples of graphene [10,11]. Direct information on the carbon electronic configuration can be extracted from the shape analysis of C KVV spectra (D parameter) excited by electron beam (AES) and X-rays (XAES) [12]. In the case of graphene, the value of D parameter is changing from graphitic one (about 21 eV) in AES to diamond-like in XAES (about 14 eV), whereas the GO samples are characterized by an intermediate value (D = 15–16 eV), which doesn't depend on the excitation source [10,11]. If the GQDs are bonded with metal ions as it happens in optical sensors [1], then the core level photoemission spectra can be used for identification of metals chemical state and for quantification of their content.

In the present study, a series of unfolded C60 fullerene samples without and with different metal ions have been prepared and analyzed by UV–Vis optical spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy and combined XPS/AES techniques to obtain the information on optical properties and chemical composition of QDs. Optical measurements of the fluorescence and absorbance in solution were performed in order to verify the possibility of using GQDs for optical sensing of heavy metals. Acquired results are supported by previously published data of transmission electron microscopy (TEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy and dynamic light scattering (DLS) obtained for the same samples [13,14].

Unfolded-fullerene graphene quantum dots were prepared starting from C60 buckminsterfullerene (Solaris Chem Inc.). The fullerene was oxidized using potassium permanganate and sulfuric acid, two strong oxidizing agents, by a modification of the classic Hummer method [9]. Briefly, sulfuric acid and sodium nitrate were mixed and stirred with the fullerene powder in a beaker for 2 h in an ice bath while potassium permanganate was added gradually. After 2 h at 0 °C, the mixture was stirred for 4 h at room temperature, then deionized water (DI, 18.2 MΩ·cm) was added and the solution was moved to an oil bath at 30 °C for 15 min. More DI was added and the solution was put into oil bath at 75 °C for 30 min to promote the oxidation process. The reaction was thus quenched with hydrogen peroxide and the pH was brought to 8. The solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag of 2000 Da with deionized water to reduce the pH to 7 and to remove the chemical reagents. After dialysis, the stock solution was diluted with deionized water to achieve the reference solution for specific measurements.

Interaction of GQDs with the metal ions was accomplished in a following way: 1.5 mL of the reference solution were mixed and stirred for 60 s with 1.5 mL of the metal salt solution of appropriate concentration. The transmittance and PL spectra were thus recorded and compared with the same spectra of the reference solution. The pH was controlled immediately before and after each measurement.

The surface analyses were carried in a spectrometer Escalab MkII (VG Scientific, East Grinstead, UK). A drop of solution with GQDs was placed on Au foil or Si wafer and dried in air at 50–60 °C. Si substrates were used only for some samples to acquire the valence band spectra without Au contribution near the Fermi edge. Al Kα source was used for the XPS and XAES, whereas a LEG-200 electron gun was used for multipoint AES measurements. Both the energy and current of the electron beam were kept very low (1–2 keV and 4–6 nA) to avoid the sample charging and damage by electron beam. The photoemission spectra were collected at 40 eV pass energy in selected-area mode A3 × 8 with a diameter of ≈1 mm, whereas the XAES spectra were registered at the pass energy of 100 eV to increase the signal-to-noise ratio. All experimental data were processed by using the software Avantage v.5 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, East Grinstead, UK). Experimental C KVV spectra were smoothed at least for 11 times by moving average routine with a width of 1.2 eV. Afterwards, these spectra were differentiated by using a width of 7 data points for the determination of D parameter.

TEM images were obtained by a FEI TECNAI 12G2 (120 keV) microscope equipped with an energy filter (GATAN GIF model) and a Peltier-cooled slow-scan charged coupled device (794 IF model). AFM was performed in tapping mode (AC) with Si tip (spring constant around 40 N/m) on freshly cleaved mica substrate after the deposition and drying of GQD aqueous solution.

Fluorescence measurements were performed by using a laboratory setup for photoluminescence (PL) equipped with a 200-W Hg(Xe) continuous discharge lamp (Oriel Corp.) followed by an excitation 25-cm monochromator (Photon Technology International, Inc., USA). The samples were investigated in rectangular fused-silica cuvettes with an optical length of 10 mm. PL signal was collected at the conventional 90° geometry by quartz lenses and an emission 25-cm monochromator (Cornerstone 260, Oriel Corp.) with appropriate rejection filters. Signal revelation was carried out by a R3896 photomultiplier (Hamamatsu Photonics K.K.). All the emission spectra were generally recorded by using the excitation wavelength of λex = 300 nm with a spectral bandwidth of 3 nm, approximately, even though standard. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by using a Cary 50 spectrophotometer (Varian, Inc., USA).

TEM images in Fig. 1 show that GQDs have quite a uniform dispersion without apparent aggregation with the size of the single nanoparticles in the range from 1 to 2.5 nm, as expected from cage-opening of single fullerenes. On the other hand, AFM images in Fig. 2 revealed the particles with lateral dimensions of about 15 nm and height of about 0.5–2 nm, suggesting that the aggregation of few GQDs occurred on mica substrate during solvent evaporation.

Typical spectra of C 1 s region acquired for the samples of pure GQDs and GQDs with Cu ions are presented in Fig. 3 . These spectra are composed of three carbon species which were identical in all the samples: prevalent asymmetric peak of CC bonds with binding energy (BE) of 284.6 eV, CO bonds at BE = 286.4 eV and carboxylic bonds at BE = 288.6 eV. All these species of carbon are present in the samples of graphene [12] and GO [10,11], but in the case of GO both C 1 s components at higher BE are much stronger. The presence of Cu ions (Fig. 3b) doesn't change the shape of C 1 s spectrum, also in the samples with other metal ions only the ratios of C2 and C3 components were slightly modified. From the intensity ratio of C 1 s peak with the signal from substrate (Au 4f or Si 2p) can be calculated the thickness of carbon overlayer. This calculation was carried out by using the Beer-Lambert model of a single overlayer with inelastic mean free path in carbon of 1.06 nm [12]. In the samples of pure GQDs, the thickness of carbon overlayer was about 0.6 nm, which is higher than that of single layer graphene (0.335 nm), but still is very reasonable having in mind the influence of contaminants, the roughness of substrate and non-uniform distribution of very small GQDs. Obtained thickness value is confirmed by AFM data [14], where the height of the particles was in the range of 0.5–2 nm, while their agglomerates on mica substrate had the lateral dimensions of about 15 nm. The lateral dimensions of single GQDs determined from TEM images were from 1 to 2.5 nm and were in good agreement with hydrodynamic radius of 3.5 nm measured by DLS [13]. When the metal ions were added into solution, the TEM and DLS measurements revealed the formation of much larger aggregates of the nanoparticles with dimensions in the range of 100–400 nm, depending on the metal ions [13].

The assessment of carbon electronic configuration in the GQDs samples without and with metal ions was carried out by analysis of C KVV spectra acquired by using X-ray photons (XAES) and electron beam (AES). The comparison of both C KVV spectra in derivative form for pure GQDs sample is reported in Fig. 4 . As can be seen from this figure, the D parameter is changing from 13.4 eV (diamond-like) in XAES to 23 eV (graphitic) in AES, exactly akin to reference samples of graphene [12]. The values of D parameter determined for all investigated samples together with literature data for reference samples [12] are reported in Table 1 . These values are only slightly changing in the range of 12.6–15.4 eV, indicating that the bonding with different metal ions and consequent aggregation of GQDs [13] don't modify the prevalent configuration of carbon corresponding to graphene. The range of D parameter for AES corresponds to 5 different points analyzed on the sample surface. The dominant presence of graphene is confirmed also by the XPS valence band spectra of GQDs samples without and with Cu, which are shown in Fig. 5 . The absence of occupied states near to the Fermi edge (no photoemission signal till BE ≈ 3 eV) is characteristic for graphene [12,15].

The species of oxygen present in the samples were revealed by analyzing O 1 s spectra illustrated in Fig. 6 . Typical two components of these spectra were positioned at BE = 531.6–531.8 eV and BE = 533.0–533.3 eV and can be attributed to hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, respectively. Of course, some contribution of CO bonds can be also present in the first component and adsorbed water in the second one. It should be noted that the total atomic ratio of C:O in GQDs samples was about 3, i.e. over the upper value of this ratio in GO [6,16]. The atomic ratios of both oxygen components O2:O1 in different samples are shown in Fig. 7a. In all the samples with metal ions, this ratio is higher than in pure GQDs, indicating the probable bonding of metal ions with carboxylic groups present on the borders of GQDs. Relative content of different metal ions was evaluated by plotting their atomic ratios with the main component of C 1 s spectra (CC bonds). This comparison for the samples with 100 μM concentration of metal ions is presented in Fig. 7b, where atomic ratio in different samples is varying from 0.06 (Hg2+) to 0.14 (Cd2+). The increase of metals concentration up to 1000 μM in solution led to their precipitation, while the atomic ratio M: C1 in solution remained about the same as in Fig. 5b.

The chemical state of metals M2+ was identified in all the samples with exception of the sample with As, where the presence of As3+ ions [17] was revealed from As 3d spectrum shown in Fig. 8a. In addition to As 3d spin-orbit doublet with As 3d5/2 peak at BE = 44.3 eV, in this spectrum is present the peak of Mn 3p due to the residuals of fullerene oxidation. The chemical state of As3+ was also confirmed by the position of high energy peak As 2p3/2 at BE = 1326.0 eV. Only in the samples with Cu2+ ions in addition to the oxide CuO component at BE = 933.4 eV was revealed the presence of hydroxide Cu(OH)2 at BE = 935.5 eV, which was even higher than oxide component (see Fig. 8b).

The UV–vis optical absorption spectrum and the fluorescence emission spectrum of water suspensions of pure GQDs are displayed in Fig. 9 (marked as 0 M). These optical characteristics are substantially similar to those found in regular GQDs either synthesized by a top-down method from graphite or using a bottom-up synthesis from citric acid [1,5,7]. In particular, the absorption spectrum exhibited a smooth shoulder at 305 nm which is generally attributed to the n-π* transition in the CO bonds of oxygen-containing functional groups, i.e. carboxyl, hydroxyl and epoxy moieties, dangling from the carbon structure. On the other hand, the present GQDs showed some peculiar differences in the interaction with heavy metal ions, possibly owing to the different lattice structure. Specifically, the addition of either Cu2+ produced a typical fluorescence quenching effect [18] (see Fig. 9a, colored curves) along with a progressive increase of the optical absorbance (Fig. 9b, colored curves). Similar fluorescence quenching has been observed with Pb2+ (see [13,14]), which also produced precipitation with removal of the metal from the solution above a concentration of 100 μM. In addition, with both ionic species, the quenched-fluorescence lifetime did not show noticeable variations in comparison with the reference signal (see ref. [14]). This behavior is generally considered as the optical fingerprint of a static fluorescence quenching [18,19], i.e. due to the formation of stable, ground-state nonfluorescent GOQ-metal complexes. Specifically, the metal ions could bind to the carbon nanoparticles through the carboxyl groups located at their edges, possibly promoting an edge-to-edge aggregation of GQDs. Nonradiative processes would thus arise and quench the fluorescent emission. On the other hand, Cd2+ did not produce any appreciable variations of absorbance but gave rise to a remarkable enhancement of fluorescence intensity (Fig. 10 , colored curves). This behavior has never been observed with carbon nanoparticles and resembles the fluorescence turn-on effect frequently found in molecular fluorophores upon chelation of cadmium. It is generally referred to as “chelation-enhanced fluorescence” (CHEF) and attributed to a peculiar mechanism of interaction. Briefly, Cd2+ ions form a dative bond with the carbonyl oxygen and immobilize its lone pair electrons that, in the absence of the metal ion, would instead fill the empty states left by the optical excitation and reduce the radiative recombination processes of the fluorescent ligand [20]. In the presence of As3+, the GQDs exhibited a further different behavior, with a fluorescence quenching effect, but without any variations of absorbance (not shown), possibly due to the fact that As is supposed to bind with the surface of GQDs, rather than with the edge [21]. In all cases, the fluorescence lifetime remained unchanged and equal to that of pure GQDs [14]. While all these findings strongly support the hypothesis of the formation of stable ground-state GOQ-metal complexes, they also suggest that the existence of different complex configurations must be assumed in order to explain the variety of the observed response. To this end, a more refined model should consider the possibility of different metal ions binding to distinct locations of GQDs [22]. Therefore, further investigations are required also in a view of possible applications to detection of heavy metals in water. In fact, the diversification of the optical response could enable the discrimination of ionic species through simple fluorescence and absorption measurements. This characteristic could be very useful for the implementation of selective optical sensors.

Simple and effective method to produce graphene-like nanoplatelets (GQDs) by unfolding buckminsterfullerene was developed. The dominant presence of graphene-like carbon in GQDs was proved by XPS and AES characterization. C KVV spectra acquired by XAES were employed for the recognition of graphene, which is characterized by the diamond-like value of D = 13–14 eV. This was confirmed also by graphene feature in the valence band: the absence of occupied states near to the Fermi level. Metal ions were probably bonded via carboxylic groups on the borders of GQDs. Atomic ratio of metal ions with graphene was dependent on the metals with a maximal one of 0.14 for Cd2+.

Different types of fluorescence response (enhancement and quenching) were detected for GQDs with different metal ions. The possibility of optical detection and identification of diverse metal ions in water by using GQDs was demonstrated experimentally.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was partially supported by Italian research project n. 85-2017-15125 funded by Regione Lazio according to L.R.13/08.
